intro.kicker: Topic 21 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Key facts and conceptual highlights for friction as tested in NEET. Core rules: friction is independent of contact area but depends on the nature of surfaces and the normal reaction. The ordering μ_rolling < μ_sliding < μ_static must be memorised. Friction is a non-conservative force (energy dissipated as heat). Static friction is self-adjusting up to its maximum value (μ_s N). Kinetic friction is constant regardless of speed, while rolling friction is smallest. These conceptual rules appear as NEET MCQs frequently.

expectedQuestions.description: NEET conceptual question: true or false — friction depends on area of contact; correct order of coefficients; whether friction does positive or negative work; what friction depends on. Also tested: angle of repose derivation, condition for block not to slide. These tips consolidate all chapter-05 conceptual rules in one topic.

timeRequired.description: Review all conceptual rules in 30 min. This topic consolidates the chapter’s conceptual rules. No new formulas; focus is on recognising statements that are true vs false and applying the correct ranking μ_rolling < μ_sliding < μ_static in novel contexts.

difficulty.description: Conceptual — no heavy computation. Common traps: thinking friction depends on area (it does NOT); thinking kinetic friction increases with speed (it does NOT in standard model); confusing static and kinetic friction in equilibrium problems. These are quick 1-mark NEET gains.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers most friction concepts but uses slightly different emphasis. NEET specifically tests the comparison μ_rolling < μ_sliding < μ_static and the non-conservative nature of friction more directly as standalone questions. NRI students should make sure to memorise the ordering and recall that friction force is always tangential to the contact surface (opposing relative motion).

weightage.sectionTitle: Tips and Tricks — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Conceptual friction rules appear in 3 of 6 recent years.
μ ordering and area-independence are the most common test points.
Non-conservative: friction can do positive, negative, or zero work depending on frame — a NEET favourite nuance.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the μ Ordering
strategySteps.description: μ_rolling < μ_sliding (kinetic) < μ_static. Rolling friction is smallest (ball/wheel rolls on surface). Static friction is the largest category. Among static, kinetic, and rolling: rolling < kinetic < static. Always. This order is directly testable.

strategySteps.title: Area-Independence Rule
strategySteps.description: Friction does NOT depend on the area of contact. F_friction = μN. Period. NEET often presents statements like ‘increasing contact area increases friction’ — this is FALSE. The reason: more area ⇒ less pressure per unit area ⇒ same total N ⇒ same friction.

strategySteps.title: Non-Conservative Nature
strategySteps.description: Friction is non-conservative: path matters; energy is dissipated as heat. Friction can do negative work (when acting opposite to displacement), positive work (e.g., friction on a book in an accelerating car), or even zero work (static friction when surface is not moving). NEET tests all three cases.

cards.title: Friction Quick-Reference Summary Sheet
cards.description: All friction tips: μ ordering, area-independence, non-conservative rule, self-adjusting static friction, angle of repose = angle of friction, pseudo-force friction applications. One-page cheat-sheet for last-day NEET revision.

cards.title: Chapter 05 Friction — Complete Concept Map
cards.description: Mind-map linking all 22 topics of friction: types, laws, angle of repose, coefficients, stopping distances, rotor, two-block problems, and tips. Use on NEET exam day for rapid recall.

cards.title: Friction: 10 NEET Conceptual Rules
cards.coreRevision: 1) Friction is non-conservative (dissipates energy as heat). 2) μ ordering: μ_rolling < μ_sliding < μ_static. 3) Friction is independent of area of contact. 4) Friction depends on the nature of the surfaces and the normal reaction. 5) Static friction is self-adjusting (0 to μ_s N). 6) Kinetic friction is ~constant, independent of speed. 7) f_limiting = μ_s N (maximum static friction). 8) f_kinetic = μ_k N. 9) Angle of friction = angle of repose = arctan(μ). 10) Friction can act in any tangential direction, not just opposing motion (e.g., static friction drives car forward).
points: Friction force ≠ f(area): depends only on N and surface nature
Static friction is self-adjusting up to μ_s N
Kinetic friction is constant (independent of speed in simple model)
cards.example: NEET MCQ: friction between block and surface is doubled if contact area is doubled. Answer: FALSE. Friction = μN; N is unchanged, area is unchanged in its effect on friction; F_friction unchanged.

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on friction conceptual rules.

gaps.title: μ Ordering Less Emphasised in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics covers static and kinetic friction but rarely demands memorisation of the ordering μ_rolling < μ_sliding < μ_static as a standalone test item. NEET frequently presents statements like ‘rolling friction is the largest type’ (FALSE) or ‘static friction is always greater than kinetic friction’ (generally TRUE). NRI students should commit this ordering to memory.
bullets: Rolling < Kinetic < Static: the order of increasing coefficient of friction.
Area-independence: pressing surface harder does NOT increase friction (only increasing N does).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about friction is CORRECT?

options.text: Friction force depends on the area of contact between surfaces
Rolling friction is greater than sliding friction
Kinetic friction is independent of the speed of the object (standard model)
Static friction is always equal to μ_s N

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. In the standard model, kinetic friction μ_k N is independent of speed. Option A is false (friction is area-independent). Option B is false (μ_rolling < μ_sliding). Option D is false (static friction is self-adjusting from 0 to μ_s N; it equals μ_s N ONLY at the point of impending slipping).

questions.question: The correct ordering of coefficients of friction is:
questions.explanation: μ_static > μ_kinetic > μ_rolling. Static is the hardest to overcome (largest μ). Rolling is the easiest (smallest μ). This is confirmed by: tyres locked vs rolling on a road, or ball rolling on a surface. NEET direct recall.

questions.question: Friction depends on which of the following?
options: Nature of surfaces and normal reaction only
questions.answer: Nature of surfaces and normal reaction only

questions.question: A block is pushed on a surface at twice the area. Friction force will:
Friction is classified as a ______ force:
Which friction type is smallest for the same normal force?

items.title: Memorise μ ordering
items.description: μ_rolling < μ_kinetic < μ_static. Tested directly as MCQ. Rolling is always the smallest; static is always the largest among the three common types.

items.title: Confirm area-independence
items.description: F_friction = μN. The area of contact does NOT appear in this formula. Greater area ⇒ less pressure but same total normal force N ⇒ same friction. This is one of the most commonly tested friction misconceptions in NEET.

items.title: Apply non-conservative rule
items.description: Friction dissipates energy to heat; it is path-dependent. It can do positive work (static friction on a book in an accelerating car), negative work (kinetic friction opposing sliding), or zero work (static friction when object doesn’t slip). Be ready for all three in NEET conceptual questions.

items.question: Why is friction independent of area of contact?
items.answer: Microscopically, friction arises from adhesion at actual contact points (asperities). A larger nominal area does not increase the number of actual contact asperities — the same weight is distributed over a larger area with lower pressure per asperity. The result is the same total friction force. Phenomenologically: F = μN. The area A does not appear, confirming independence.

items.question: Can friction do positive work?
items.answer: Yes. Friction can do positive work when it acts in the direction of displacement. Example: a book resting on an accelerating truck. Static friction from the truck floor pulls the book forward, doing positive work on the book (increasing its KE). Friction is non-conservative but not always ‘negative’ in terms of work done.

items.question: Why is μ_rolling < μ_sliding?
items.answer: Rolling contact involves microscopic deformation and recovery cycles rather than sustained sliding. The energy lost per contact area in rolling is much less than in sliding (where all asperities are continuously sheared). Hence the effective friction coefficient for rolling is much smaller. Ball bearings and tyres exploit this: rolling reduces friction compared to a sliding block.

grounding_summary: Page 244 includes summary remarks on friction as a non-conservative force, the relationship between coefficients (mu_rolling < mu_sliding < mu_static), and the area-independence of friction. These are stated as key tips at the chapter close.
The textbook connects these conceptual rules to the numerical problems preceding them, providing context for NEET-focused rule memorisation.
grounding_excerpt: the person remains stuck to the wall even when the floor is removed, because friction force balances its weight in this condition.
When two masses compressed towards each other and suddenly released then energy acquired by each block will be dissipated against friction and finally block comes to rest
By the law of conservation of energy
